content.text: What Conventional Commits Add for GenAI Repos

Conventional Commits is a lightweight specification that structures commit messages as machine-readable tokens: type(scope): description. For GenAI application repositories, structured commit messages enable two things beyond conventional software: automated changelog generation that distinguishes prompt changes from code changes, and filtering the git log by the type of GenAI asset changed (prompt, tool, eval, guardrail).

The standard commit types (feat, fix, refactor, test, docs) cover code changes well, but GenAI repos contain assets that do not map cleanly onto these categories. A system prompt rewrite is not a 'feat' and not a 'fix' — it is a behavioral change to a prompt, which deserves its own type.

attrs.alt: Conventional commit anatomy: type(scope): description with body and footer trailers

content.text: Extended Type Tokens for GenAI
Type token
When to use
Example commit subject
prompt
System prompt or user-turn template changes
prompt(summarization): add length constraint to output instructions
guardrail
Adding, modifying, or removing a content filter or refusal rule
guardrail(pii-filter): block SSN patterns in search results
tool
Tool schema definition changes (name, description, parameters)
tool(web-search): add max_results parameter with default 3
agent
Agent orchestration topology, routing rules, or config
agent(router): add fallback to retrieval agent on tool failure
eval
Eval dataset additions, modifications, or evaluator changes
eval(date-extraction): add adversarial cases for relative dates
feat
New application feature (code, not prompt/tool)
feat(chat-ui): add message history sidebar
fix
Bug fix in application code (not a prompt behavioral fix)
fix(json-parser): handle null content field in tool response
refactor
Code restructuring with no behavioral change
refactor(llm-client): extract retry logic to separate module
perf
Performance improvement
perf(embedding-cache): switch to Redis for shared cache
test
Test additions without production code change
test(tool-schema): add schema validation unit tests
hotfix
Emergency production fix (use only on hotfix branches)
hotfix(safety): reject empty queries in web_search tool
Commit Message Format and Rules

# Full format:
# <type>(<scope>): <description>
#
# [optional body]
#
# [optional footers]

# Rules:
# 1. Subject line <= 72 characters
# 2. Subject line is imperative present tense: "add" not "added" or "adds"
# 3. No period at the end of the subject line
# 4. Body (if present) separated from subject by one blank line
# 5. Footers are key: value pairs, each on its own line
# 6. BREAKING CHANGE footer (all caps) required when downstream behavior changes

# GenAI-specific footer trailers:
# Eval-case: <id>[, <id>]   — eval case IDs that exercise the changed behavior
# Safety-reviewed-by: @<handle>  — required for prompt, guardrail, tool changes
# Flag: <flag-name>          — feature flag name controlling this change
Scope Conventions
The scope token (in parentheses after the type) identifies which part of the application the commit affects. Consistent scopes make it practical to filter the git log by topic.

# Filter git log by type and scope
git log --oneline --grep="^prompt" -- src/prompts/   # all prompt changes
git log --oneline --grep="^guardrail"                  # all guardrail changes
git log --oneline --grep="^eval"                        # all eval additions

# List all commits affecting the date-extraction feature
git log --oneline --grep="(date-extraction)"
Scope
What it covers
system-prompt
The top-level system prompt file
user-template
User turn templates or few-shot examples
<feature-name>
e.g. date-extraction, calendar-tool — the feature this change is part of
<tool-name>
e.g. web-search, calculator — the tool whose schema is changing
routing
Agent routing or orchestration logic
eval-harness
The evaluation framework itself (not dataset changes)
ci
CI/CD pipeline configuration
The BREAKING CHANGE Footer

A BREAKING CHANGE footer signals that the commit changes behavior in a way that breaks downstream consumers: API clients, dependent services, or existing integrations. For GenAI repos, breaking changes include:
Output format changes:
switching from freeform text to JSON, or changing a JSON key name
Tool parameter renames:
renaming a parameter that callers currently supply
Scope restrictions:
a guardrail that now refuses queries that previously succeeded — callers may need to handle the new refusal case
Model switch:
switching the underlying model version may change output style, length, or capability even for identical prompts

# Example commit with BREAKING CHANGE:
tool(web-search): rename query parameter to search_query

The parameter was named 'query' which conflicted with the reserved
'query' key in our internal request schema. Renaming to 'search_query'
removes the ambiguity.

BREAKING CHANGE: callers supplying 'query' must update to 'search_query'.
The old parameter name will be rejected by the updated schema.
Eval-case: tool-schema-003
Safety-reviewed-by: @alice
Enforcing Commit Format with a Pre-Commit Hook

A commit-msg hook validates the commit message before it is recorded. This prevents malformed commits from entering the branch history and reduces review friction when the CI commit parser encounters unexpected formats.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# .git/hooks/commit-msg
# Install: chmod +x .git/hooks/commit-msg

COMMIT_MSG_FILE="$1"
COMMIT_MSG=$(cat "$COMMIT_MSG_FILE")

# Conventional commit pattern
PATTERN='^(feat|fix|refactor|test|docs|perf|chore|build|ci|style|prompt|guardrail|tool|agent|eval|hotfix)(\([a-z0-9-]+\))?(!)?: .{1,72}$'

# Check only the subject line (first line)
SUBJECT=$(echo "$COMMIT_MSG" | head -1)

if ! echo "$SUBJECT" | grep -qE "$PATTERN"; then
  echo "ERROR: Commit message does not follow Conventional Commits format."
  echo "  Expected: <type>(<scope>): <description>"
  echo "  Valid types: feat fix refactor test docs perf chore build ci style prompt guardrail tool agent eval hotfix"
  echo "  Got: $SUBJECT"
  exit 1
fi

# GenAI-specific: prompt/guardrail/tool/agent changes require Safety-reviewed-by footer
if echo "$SUBJECT" | grep -qE '^(prompt|guardrail|tool|agent)'; then
  if ! echo "$COMMIT_MSG" | grep -q "Safety-reviewed-by:"; then
    echo "WARNING: prompt/guardrail/tool/agent commits should include 'Safety-reviewed-by: @handle' footer"
    # Warning only — not a hard block, since solo dev sessions may not have a reviewer yet
  fi
fi

exit 0

The hook above is a warning, not a hard block, for the safety reviewer footer — because during development a developer works alone and does not have a reviewer. The CODEOWNERS review in CI is the enforced gate; the hook is a reminder.
Generating Changelogs from Commit History

With structured commit messages, changelog generation becomes a script rather than a manual task. The following script produces a changelog grouped by commit type, with GenAI-specific sections for prompt, guardrail, and tool changes.

# scripts/generate_changelog.py
import subprocess, re, sys
from collections import defaultdict

def git_log(since: str, until: str = "HEAD") -> list[str]:
    return subprocess.check_output(
        ["git", "log", f"{since}..{until}", "--oneline", "--no-merges"],
        text=True
    ).splitlines()

SECTIONS = {
    "prompt":    "Prompt Changes",
    "guardrail": "Guardrail Changes",
    "tool":      "Tool Schema Changes",
    "agent":     "Agent Changes",
    "eval":      "Eval Updates",
    "feat":      "New Features",
    "fix":       "Bug Fixes",
    "hotfix":    "Emergency Fixes",
    "perf":      "Performance",
    "refactor":  "Refactoring",
}

PATTERN = re.compile(r'^([a-f0-9]+) (\w+)(\([^)]+\))?: (.+)$')

def parse_commits(lines: list[str]) -> dict[str, list[tuple[str, str, str]]]:
    grouped = defaultdict(list)
    for line in lines:
        m = PATTERN.match(line)
        if not m:
            continue
        sha, typ, scope, desc = m.groups()
        scope = scope[1:-1] if scope else ""
        grouped[typ].append((sha, scope, desc))
    return grouped

def render_changelog(since: str, until: str = "HEAD") -> str:
    lines = git_log(since, until)
    groups = parse_commits(lines)
    parts = [f"## Changelog {since}..{until}\n"]
    for type_key, section_title in SECTIONS.items():
        commits = groups.get(type_key, [])
        if not commits:
            continue
        parts.append(f"\n### {section_title}\n")
        for sha, scope, desc in commits:
            scope_str = f"**{scope}**: " if scope else ""
            parts.append(f"- {scope_str}{desc} ({sha[:7]})")
    return "\n".join(parts)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    since = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "v1.0.0"
    print(render_changelog(since))
Common Commit Message Mistakes
Mistake
Bad example
Correct form
Wrong type for a prompt change
feat(system): improve date extraction
prompt(date-extraction): improve date format instructions
Past tense in description
prompt(calendar): added ISO date requirement
prompt(calendar): add ISO date requirement
Missing scope for a specific feature
tool: add max_results parameter
tool(web-search): add max_results parameter with default 3
Body explains what, not why
prompt(router): change routing logic\n\nChanged routing to use keyword matching
prompt(router): switch to keyword matching\n\nPrevious regex-based routing failed on multi-intent queries; keyword matching has 94% accuracy on the eval set
No BREAKING CHANGE footer when behavior changes
tool(search): rename query to search_query
tool(search): rename query to search_query\n\nBREAKING CHANGE: callers must update parameter name
Commit Granularity: One Change Per Commit

Conventional Commits work best when each commit represents one atomic change. For GenAI repos, this means separating a prompt change from a tool schema change even if they are part of the same feature, because they involve different reviewers (prompt team vs. tool schema owners) and different CI gates.

# Bad: one commit mixes a prompt change with a tool schema change
# git commit -m "feat(calendar): add calendar integration"
# (mixes prompt, tool schema, and routing changes in one commit — hard to revert,
# hard to cherry-pick, requires both prompt and tool reviewers on one commit)

# Good: three atomic commits
git add src/tools/calendar_read.json
git commit -m "tool(calendar-read): add calendar_read tool schema"

git add src/prompts/system_v2_4.txt
git commit -m "prompt(system): add calendar usage instruction to assistant prompt"

git add src/agent_configs/routing.json
git commit -m "agent(routing): route scheduling queries to calendar-enabled path"

The three-commit approach has a practical benefit beyond reviewability: if the calendar tool schema causes a production issue, you can cherry-pick a revert of exactly that commit without touching the prompt or routing config.
Linking Commits to Eval Cases

The Eval-case footer trailer creates a queryable link between a commit and the eval cases that test its behavior. When an eval case fails in CI, you can quickly find which commit introduced the behavior being tested.

An eval case with no associated commit is a coverage gap: the case exists but it is not clear which feature it is testing. Running this script during a release audit catches orphaned eval cases that may be testing behavior that no longer exists.
Conventional Commits in CI: Automated Semantic Version Bumping
Semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH) can be derived automatically from commit history using the type tokens:
BREAKING CHANGE footer → MAJOR bump:
feat, prompt, tool, agent, guardrail → MINOR bump:
fix, hotfix, refactor, perf, eval → PATCH bump:
Automated version bumping removes the manual step of deciding whether a release is major, minor, or patch — a decision that is often made inconsistently across team members when left to judgment.
Adopting Conventional Commits Incrementally
A team that has been using free-form commit messages cannot switch to Conventional Commits overnight without disrupting their workflow. An incremental adoption path:
Week 1 — Educate and document:
share the type token table; add the commit-msg hook as a warning (not an error); document the scope conventions for this specific repo
Week 2 — Enforce in CI:
add a CI check that warns (not fails) on non-conforming commits; this surfaces the scope of non-compliance without blocking merges
Week 3 — Convert warnings to errors for new files:
enforce the format for changes to prompts/, tools/, and agent_configs/ where the domain-specific types matter most
Week 4+ — Full enforcement:
make the CI check a merge blocker for all files; update the PR template to remind authors of the format

# CI check during incremental adoption (week 2 — warn only)
commit-format-check:
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  continue-on-error: true   # do not block merges during adoption
  steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      with:
        fetch-depth: 0
    - name: Check commit messages
      run: |
        VIOLATIONS=0
        for sha in $(git log origin/main..HEAD --format="%H"); do
          MSG=$(git log -1 --format="%s" "$sha")
          if ! echo "$MSG" | grep -qE '^(feat|fix|prompt|guardrail|tool|agent|eval|hotfix|refactor|test|docs|perf|chore|build|ci|style)(\([a-z0-9-]+\))?: .{1,72}$'; then
            echo "NON-CONFORMING: $sha $MSG"
            VIOLATIONS=$((VIOLATIONS + 1))
          fi
        done
        echo "$VIOLATIONS non-conforming commit(s) found"
        # Exit 1 to block (change continue-on-error to false in week 4)
        exit 0   # warn only during adoption
Summary

Conventional Commits for GenAI repos extend the standard specification with additional type tokens that reflect the unique assets in an AI application: prompt, guardrail, tool, agent, eval, and hotfix. These tokens make the git log filterable by asset type, enable automated changelog generation with GenAI-specific sections, and provide metadata (Eval-case, Safety-reviewed-by) that links commit history to the test suite and review record. A commit-msg hook and CI validation enforce the format without blocking emergency hotfixes.
Practical test:

if running git log --grep='^prompt' produces a meaningful list of all prompt changes, your commit conventions are working. If it returns nothing or random results, the team is not following the format consistently.
